title: Bendigo deal on union’s hit list
full_text:
Hundreds of Bendigo Bank technology workers are facing the stripping of basic workplace rights under employment contracts proposed by Indian tech giant Infosys – with unions warning the arrangements could set a dangerous precedent.
Bendigo Bank last month announced it would transfer hundreds of Australian tech workers to Infosys as part of a “productivity program”. But the Finance Sector Union (FSU) says the contracts workers are being handed are far worse than anticipated.
Under the proposed agreements, Infosys could force employees to work wherever it chooses and for whatever hours it considers reasonable.
Workers’ pay could go backwards in the event of a pandemic, recession, calamity or change in market conditions – with no guaranteed wage increases or pay reviews offered in return.
The contracts would also allow Infosys to demand employees hand over sensitive medical information when they are ill and could terminate workers on medical grounds.
The FSU is advising its members not to sign the contracts.
Bendigo Bank boss Richard Fennell said the changes would not be easy, but the move was expected to significantly improve the bank’s IT service delivery capability. “Partnerships are fundamental to how Bendigo Bank innovates in technology,” Mr Fennell said.
